name: Ecuador
tagline: From the Valdivia potters and the Quitu-Cara of the high Andes to a northern Inca capital, the Real Audiencia of Quito, the first cry of independence in 1809, the victory at Pichincha in 1822, and the separate republic born in 1830 on the line of the Equator.
summary: Ecuador's history reaches back more than five thousand years to the Valdivia culture of its Pacific coast, whose fired-clay figurines are among the oldest ceramics known in the Americas. In the highlands the Quitu and Cara peoples built the chiefdoms around Quito that the Inca, advancing from the south under Topa Inca Yupanqui and Huayna Capac, absorbed in the late fifteenth century, making Quito a northern seat of empire and the base of the last Inca ruler, Atahualpa. Spanish conquest followed in the 1530s, and the colonial Real Audiencia of Quito became famous for the Quito school of religious art before Quito's criollos raised the first cry of independence on 10 August 1809, earning the city the title Luz de America. Antonio Jose de Sucre secured that independence at the Battle of Pichincha on 24 May 1822; after eight years inside Simon Bolivar's Gran Colombia, Ecuador emerged as a separate republic in 1830, and through cacao, banana and oil booms, chronic political instability, the dollarisation of 2000 and a recent security crisis it has made its way to the present.

founding.label: Republic of Ecuador separates from Gran Colombia
founding.detail: On 13 May 1830 an assembly of notables in Quito resolved that the Distrito del Sur, the southern district of Simon Bolivar's Gran Colombia, would separate and form an independent state, naming the Venezuelan-born general Juan Jose Flores supreme civil and military chief. A constituent assembly meeting at Riobamba from August 1830 drafted the first constitution of the Republic of the Equator (Republica del Ecuador) and confirmed Flores as the new nation's first president. The republic inherited the territory and identity of the colonial Real Audiencia of Quito, whose independence had been won at the Battle of Pichincha in 1822, and took its name from the Equator that crosses the country near its capital.

quickFacts.label: Oldest cultures
quickFacts.value: Valdivia ceramic culture, c. 3500-1500 BCE, among the earliest pottery in the Americas

quickFacts.label: Inca conquest
quickFacts.value: Late 15th century, under Topa Inca Yupanqui and Huayna Capac; Quito a northern Inca capital

quickFacts.label: First cry of independence
quickFacts.value: 10 August 1809 in Quito, the Luz de America

quickFacts.label: Independence secured
quickFacts.value: Battle of Pichincha, 24 May 1822, under Antonio Jose de Sucre

quickFacts.label: Republic founded
quickFacts.value: 1830, on separation from Gran Colombia under Juan Jose Flores

eras.title: Valdivia Potters and the Chiefdoms of the Andes
eras.standfirst: For thousands of years before the Inca, the land that would become Ecuador held some of the Americas' oldest ceramic traditions on the coast and a patchwork of farming chiefdoms in the Andean valleys, among them the Quitu and Cara around the future site of Quito.
body: Along the Pacific coast of the Santa Elena peninsula, between roughly 3500 and 1500 BCE, the Valdivia culture produced fired-clay vessels and small female figurines that rank among the oldest pottery known in the Western Hemisphere. Discovered in the 1950s by the Ecuadorian archaeologist Emilio Estrada and excavated at sites such as Real Alto, Valdivia reveals settled village life, agriculture and long-distance exchange far earlier than once believed. The coast, the highland Sierra and the Amazonian Oriente each developed distinct ways of life, linked by trade routes that carried the prized spondylus shell, cotton and salt across very different worlds.

Over the following millennia the highlands filled with chiefdoms supported by intensive raised-field and terrace farming. By the early second millennium CE the region around the future capital was home to the Quitu, occupants of the site of modern Quito, and to the Cara to the north, whose lords are remembered in tradition as forming a Kingdom of Quito. To the south, around present-day Cuenca, the Cañari built a powerful confederation. These were not a single state but a mosaic of rival polities, joined by commerce and often by war, with their own languages, shrines and ruling lineages.

This deep pre-Inca past is the foundation of Ecuadorian identity. The Valdivia figurines, the goldwork and the great earthen mounds of later coastal peoples such as the Manteño testify to societies of real sophistication, and the names of the Quitu, the Cara and the Cañari survive in the country's geography and memory. When the Inca armies finally pushed north from Peru in the late fifteenth century, they entered a land already long settled, deeply farmed and fiercely defended.

events.title: The Valdivia ceramic culture
events.summary: On the Pacific coast the Valdivia people make fired-clay vessels and figurines among the oldest pottery in the Americas, evidence of early settled, farming village life.

events.title: Quitu, Cara and Cañari chiefdoms
events.summary: Highland peoples including the Quitu and Cara around Quito and the Cañari near Cuenca build farming chiefdoms linked by trade and rivalry.

pullquote.text: The clay figurines of Valdivia are among the oldest pottery ever made in the Americas.
pullquote.attribution: On the antiquity of Ecuador's coastal cultures

eras.title: The Inca Conquest and the Last Emperor
eras.standfirst: In the late fifteenth century the Inca pushed north from Peru and absorbed the highland chiefdoms after fierce resistance, making Quito a northern capital of their empire; it was from here that Atahualpa fought the civil war that left him master of the Inca world on the eve of the Spanish arrival.
body: Inca expansion into the northern Andes was begun by the emperor Topa Inca Yupanqui, who reigned from about 1471 to 1493, and completed by his son Huayna Capac, who ruled until about 1525. The advance met determined resistance, above all from the Cañari around Cuenca and from the peoples around Quito, but by the close of the fifteenth century the highlands of present-day Ecuador had been incorporated into Tawantinsuyu, the Inca empire. Quito grew into a major northern centre, linked to Cuzco by the imperial road and adorned with temples, storehouses and administrative compounds.

Huayna Capac spent much of his reign in the north and died, probably of an epidemic disease spreading ahead of the Europeans, around 1525-1527. His death triggered a war of succession between two of his sons: Atahualpa, associated with Quito and the northern armies, and Huascar, the heir based at Cuzco. The struggle ravaged the empire's cities, economy and people. Early in 1532 Atahualpa's seasoned northern forces decisively defeated Huascar's army, captured him, and left Atahualpa supreme just as a small band of Spaniards under Francisco Pizarro was landing on the coast.

The collision came at Cajamarca. On 16 November 1532 the Spaniards seized Atahualpa in a surprise attack and held him for an enormous ransom of gold and silver. Although the ransom was paid, Pizarro had the emperor put on trial and executed in 1533, breaking the central authority of the Inca state. For Ecuador the Inca interlude was brief, perhaps two generations, yet it left a lasting mark in the Quechua language, in highland agriculture and roads, and in the memory of Atahualpa as a figure rooted in Quito and the north.

events.title: Inca advance into the northern Andes
events.summary: Topa Inca Yupanqui begins the conquest of the Ecuadorian highlands, met by fierce resistance from the Cañari and the peoples around Quito.

events.title: Huayna Capac completes the conquest
events.summary: Huayna Capac extends Inca rule over all of highland Ecuador and makes Quito a northern capital of the empire.

events.title: Atahualpa defeats Huascar
events.summary: Atahualpa's northern armies crush those of his half-brother Huascar near Cuzco, leaving him master of the Inca empire as the Spanish arrive.

events.title: Capture and execution of Atahualpa
events.summary: Seized by Pizarro at Cajamarca in November 1532 and ransomed, the last Inca emperor is tried and executed in 1533, shattering Inca authority.

figures.name: Atahualpa
figures.blurb: Son of Huayna Capac, associated with Quito and the northern armies, who won the Inca civil war against his half-brother Huascar only to be captured, ransomed and executed by Francisco Pizarro, ending the independent Inca state.

pullquote.text: Atahualpa provided the richest ransom in history, and was put to death all the same.
pullquote.attribution: On the fall of the last Inca emperor

eras.title: Conquest, Colony and the Quito School
eras.standfirst: Spaniards under Sebastian de Benalcazar founded a Spanish Quito in 1534; the Real Audiencia of Quito, established in 1563, governed the region for nearly three centuries and gave rise to the Quito school, one of the great workshops of religious art in colonial America.
body: After Atahualpa's death the Spanish moved to seize his northern base. Sebastian de Benalcazar, a lieutenant of Pizarro, marched on Quito; the Inca general Rumiñahui burned the old city rather than surrender it, and on 6 December 1534 the Spaniards founded San Francisco de Quito on the ruins. The new town became the nucleus of a colonial society built on the labour and tribute of the surrounding Indigenous communities, organised through the encomienda and, in the highlands, the textile obrajes whose woollen cloth supplied much of Spanish South America.

In 1563 King Philip II created the Real Audiencia of Quito, a high court and administrative district that gave the region a distinct identity within the Viceroyalty of Peru and, later, the Viceroyalty of New Granada. Over the colonial centuries Quito's monasteries and convents became famous for the Escuela Quiteña, the Quito school of art: a mestizo Baroque tradition of painting and polychrome wood sculpture in which European technique was fused with Indigenous craft and feeling. Masters such as the painter Miguel de Santiago and the sculptors Bernardo de Legarda and Manuel Chili, called Caspicara, made the city a leading exporter of religious images across the continent.

Colonial society was rigidly stratified by birth and colour, and its wealth rested on Indigenous and later enslaved African labour. Yet by the eighteenth century Quito also nurtured an educated criollo elite open to the ideas of the European Enlightenment. The most remarkable of them was Eugenio Espejo, a physician and writer of humble, mixed ancestry who attacked colonial backwardness, founded Quito's first newspaper and is honoured as the intellectual precursor of independence. When his ideas passed to a younger generation, the colonial order in Quito had little time left.

events.title: Spanish founding of Quito
events.summary: Sebastian de Benalcazar founds San Francisco de Quito on the ruins of the Inca city burned by Rumiñahui, establishing Spanish rule over the highlands.

events.title: Real Audiencia of Quito created
events.summary: Philip II establishes the Audiencia of Quito, a royal court and administrative district that gives the region its lasting colonial identity.

events.title: The Quito school of art
events.summary: Quito's convents produce the Escuela Quiteña, a mestizo Baroque tradition of painting and sculpture exported across colonial South America.

events.title: Espejo founds Quito's first newspaper
events.summary: The physician and writer Eugenio Espejo, precursor of independence, launches Quito's first newspaper and presses Enlightenment ideas.

figures.name: Sebastian de Benalcazar
figures.blurb: Lieutenant of Pizarro who conquered the Quito region and founded the Spanish city of Quito in 1534 before going on to campaign in the lands that became Colombia.

figures.name: Eugenio Espejo
figures.blurb: Quito's leading Enlightenment intellect, of Indigenous and mixed ancestry, who criticised colonial society, founded the city's first newspaper and inspired the patriots of 1809; honoured as the precursor of Ecuadorian independence and the father of its journalism.

pullquote.text: In the convents of Quito, European Baroque met Indigenous hands, and an art unlike any other in America was born.

eras.title: The Light of America: Independence and the Republic
eras.standfirst: Quito raised the first cry of independence in 1809, earning the title Luz de America; after years of struggle Antonio Jose de Sucre won the decisive Battle of Pichincha in 1822, joined the region to Gran Colombia, and in 1830 Ecuador separated to become a republic of its own.
body: On 10 August 1809 a group of Quito criollos, drawing on the ideas of Espejo, deposed the colonial authorities and proclaimed a governing junta loyal in name to the captive Spanish king but in fact self-governing. It was among the earliest such acts in Spanish America, and for it Quito is remembered as the Luz de America, the Light of America. The first rising was crushed, and in 1810 many of its leaders were killed in prison, but the movement it began could not be undone, and the cause of independence spread through the audiencia over the following decade.

Liberation finally came from outside, as the armies of Simon Bolivar advanced from the north and those of Jose de San Martin from the south. Bolivar's ablest general, Antonio Jose de Sucre, led a patriot army of Colombians and allied troops against the royalist forces of Field Marshal Melchor Aymerich, and on 24 May 1822, on the slopes of the Pichincha volcano above Quito, he won the battle that secured the independence of the provinces of the old Real Audiencia. Quito surrendered the next day, and the region was joined to the new Republic of Gran Colombia that Bolivar had forged from Venezuela, New Granada and Quito.

The union was short-lived. Gran Colombia was strained by vast distances, poor communications and regional rivalries, and it began to break apart almost as soon as the wars ended. In May 1830 an assembly in Quito resolved to separate the southern district and form an independent state; a constituent assembly at Riobamba drew up the first constitution and named the Venezuelan general Juan Jose Flores the new republic's first president. The country took its name from the Equator and entered independent life as a small, divided nation of highland and coast, its politics dominated for a generation by Flores and his rivals.

events.title: The first cry of independence
events.summary: Quito criollos depose the colonial authorities and form a governing junta, one of the earliest such acts in Spanish America, earning Quito the title Luz de America.

events.title: Battle of Pichincha
events.summary: Antonio Jose de Sucre defeats the royalist army on the slopes of Pichincha above Quito, securing the independence of the lands of the old Audiencia of Quito.

events.title: Union with Gran Colombia
events.summary: Liberated Quito joins Simon Bolivar's Republic of Gran Colombia alongside Venezuela and New Granada until the union breaks apart.

events.title: Republic of Ecuador founded
events.summary: An assembly in Quito separates the southern district from Gran Colombia and founds the Republic of Ecuador, with Juan Jose Flores as first president.

figures.name: Antonio Jose de Sucre
figures.blurb: Bolivar's most trusted lieutenant, who liberated Quito at the Battle of Pichincha in 1822 and went on to win independence for Peru and Bolivia; widely regarded as one of the noblest soldiers of the South American revolution.

figures.name: Juan Jose Flores
figures.blurb: Venezuelan-born general and ally of Bolivar who led Ecuador's separation from Gran Colombia in 1830 and became the republic's first president, dominating its early politics.

pullquote.text: Quito is the Luz de America, the Light of America, for it raised among the first of all the cries for freedom.

eras.title: Cacao, Liberals and Bananas
eras.standfirst: From the mid-nineteenth century Ecuador rode the export booms of cacao and then bananas, swung between the Catholic conservatism of Gabriel Garcia Moreno and the anticlerical Liberal Revolution of Eloy Alfaro in 1895, and saw Charles Darwin's Galapagos pass into legend and, later, protection.
body: The young republic's economy came to rest on the cacao of the coast around the great port of Guayaquil. Demand from a chocolate-hungry world drove a long boom, and from the 1880s into the 1910s Ecuador was the leading cacao exporter on earth, the trade making fortunes for a coastal planter and merchant elite and shifting wealth and power toward Guayaquil. Politics, meanwhile, was riven between conservatives and liberals. The conservative Gabriel Garcia Moreno, president for much of the 1860s and early 1870s, bound the state tightly to the Catholic Church before his assassination in 1875.

The liberal counter-current triumphed in the Liberal Revolution of 1895, when Eloy Alfaro, a coastal radical, seized power as supreme chief. His governments separated church and state, established secular public education, legalised civil marriage and divorce, and pushed through the railway linking Guayaquil to Quito, knitting coast and highland together. The struggle was bitter and Alfaro himself was murdered by a mob in Quito in 1912, but the liberal reforms reshaped the country. It was in this period, too, that Ecuador's offshore treasure entered the wider imagination, for it was at the Galapagos that the young Charles Darwin, arriving aboard HMS Beagle in 1835, gathered observations that would feed his theory of evolution by natural selection.

Cacao gave way to disease and depression after the First World War, and the search for a new staple ended in the banana. From the late 1940s Ecuador's coastal plantations made it, by the mid-1950s, the largest banana exporter in the world, and the trade brought a measure of prosperity and renewed the weight of the coast in national life. The country also moved at last to protect its unique islands: in 1959 the government declared the Galapagos a national park, the foundation of their later recognition as one of the first natural sites on the UNESCO World Heritage List.

events.title: Darwin reaches the Galapagos
events.summary: The naturalist Charles Darwin visits the Galapagos aboard HMS Beagle, gathering observations that help shape his theory of evolution by natural selection.

events.title: The cacao boom
events.summary: Ecuador becomes the world's leading cacao exporter, enriching the coastal elite of Guayaquil and shifting wealth and power toward the coast.

events.title: The Liberal Revolution of Eloy Alfaro
events.summary: Eloy Alfaro seizes power and launches liberal reforms separating church and state, founding secular schools and building the Guayaquil-Quito railway.

events.title: The banana boom
events.summary: Ecuador becomes the world's largest banana exporter, the fruit replacing cacao as the leading crop and renewing coastal prosperity.

events.title: Galapagos National Park
events.summary: Ecuador declares the Galapagos Islands a national park, the basis of their later inscription as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

figures.name: Eloy Alfaro
figures.blurb: Coastal radical who led the Liberal Revolution of 1895, separated church and state, founded secular education and built the Guayaquil-Quito railway before being killed by a mob in Quito in 1912.

figures.name: Gabriel Garcia Moreno
figures.blurb: Dominant conservative leader of the 1860s and early 1870s who bound the Ecuadorian state closely to the Catholic Church and modernised the country before his assassination in 1875.

pullquote.text: From the islands the young Darwin carried home the questions that would remake the understanding of life itself.
pullquote.attribution: On the Galapagos and the voyage of the Beagle, 1835

eras.title: Oil, Dollar and the Modern Crisis
eras.standfirst: Amazonian oil from 1972 transformed the economy, but chronic political instability, financial collapse and the dollarisation of 2000 marked the decades that followed; after the long Correa years the country has confronted, since 2024, a grave crisis of organised crime and violence.
body: The discovery of large oil reserves in the Amazonian Oriente turned Ecuador, with the first exports in 1972, from an agrarian economy into a petroleum exporter. Oil revenue fuelled almost a decade of rapid growth, expanded the state and financed roads, schools and cities, but it also tied the nation's fortunes to a volatile world price and deepened conflicts over the Amazon's land and peoples. Through these years government alternated between military rule and fragile civilian democracy, with a return to elected government in 1979.

The late twentieth century brought recurring instability. Falling oil prices, debt and weak institutions produced a revolving door of presidents, several of them driven from office before their terms ended, amid the 1990s rise of a powerful Indigenous movement. The crisis peaked in 1998-1999 in a collapse of the banking system and the currency; in January 2000 President Jamil Mahuad announced that Ecuador would abandon its currency, the sucre, and adopt the United States dollar. He was overthrown within days, but dollarisation remained, stabilising prices at the cost of monetary independence and binding the economy to the dollar ever since.

From 2007 the populist Rafael Correa governed for a decade of his self-styled Citizens' Revolution, using oil wealth to expand the state and rewrite the constitution before handing power to his former ally Lenin Moreno in 2017. The years since have been turbulent: as global cocaine routes shifted, Ecuador's ports and prisons were captured by drug-trafficking gangs, and homicide rates soared. In January 2024 President Daniel Noboa declared an internal armed conflict and sent the army against the cartels after a notorious gang leader escaped prison and gunmen stormed a television studio live on air. The fight against organised violence, alongside the management of a dollarised economy, defines Ecuador's present.

events.title: The oil era begins
events.summary: Ecuador begins exporting Amazonian oil, transforming its economy and government and launching nearly a decade of rapid growth.

events.title: Return to civilian rule
events.summary: After years of military government Ecuador returns to elected civilian democracy, opening an era of fragile and often interrupted constitutional rule.

events.title: Dollarisation
events.summary: Amid financial collapse President Jamil Mahuad adopts the US dollar as Ecuador's currency, abandoning the sucre; he is ousted days later but the dollar stays.

events.title: The Correa decade
events.summary: Rafael Correa governs for ten years of his Citizens' Revolution, using oil revenue to expand the state and rewrite the constitution.

events.title: Internal armed conflict declared
events.summary: President Daniel Noboa declares an internal armed conflict and deploys the military against drug-trafficking gangs after a gang leader's prison escape and televised attacks.

figures.name: Rafael Correa
figures.blurb: Economist and populist leader whose decade-long Citizens' Revolution used oil wealth to expand the state and a new 2008 constitution to reshape Ecuadorian politics.

figures.name: Jamil Mahuad
figures.blurb: President during the financial collapse of 1999 who in January 2000 replaced the sucre with the US dollar; he was driven from office within days, but dollarisation endured.

pullquote.text: Abandoning the sucre for the dollar bought price stability at the price of monetary independence.

figures.name: Atahualpa
figures.blurb: The last independent Inca ruler, rooted in Quito and the northern armies, whose capture and execution by the Spanish ended the Inca empire.

figures.name: Antonio Jose de Sucre
figures.blurb: Bolivar's foremost general, who secured Ecuador's independence at the Battle of Pichincha in 1822 and went on to liberate Peru and Bolivia.

figures.name: Eugenio Espejo
figures.blurb: Quito's Enlightenment physician and writer, founder of its first newspaper and intellectual forerunner of the independence movement of 1809.
